(Spanish pronunciation: [[sanˈtjaɣo ðe ˈkali]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Spanish "Help:IPA/Spanish")), or **Cali**, is the capital of the [Valle del Cauca department](/wiki/Valle_del_Cauca_department "Valle del Cauca department"), and the most populous city in southwest [Colombia](/wiki/Colombia "Colombia"), with an estimated 2,280,522 residents in 2023.[[5]](#cite_note-5) The city spans 560.3 km2 (216.3 sq mi) with 120.9 km2 (46.7 sq mi) of urban area,[[6]](#cite_note-6) making Cali the second-largest city in the country by area and the [third most populous](/wiki/List_of_cities_and_towns_in_Colombia "List of cities and towns in Colombia"). Cali is the main urban and economic center in the south of the country, and has one of Colombia's fastest-growing economies.[[7]](#cite_note-7)[[8]](#cite_note-8)[[9]](#cite_note-9) The city was founded on 25 July 1536 by the Spanish explorer [Sebastián de Belalcázar](/wiki/Sebasti%C3%A1n_de_Belalc%C3%A1zar "Sebastián de Belalcázar").

**Santiago de Cali** (Spanish pronunciation: [[sanˈtjaɣo ðe ˈkali]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Spanish "Help:IPA/Spanish")), or **Cali**, is the capital of the [Valle del Cauca department](/wiki/Valle_del_Cauca_department "Valle del Cauca department"), and the most populous city in southwest [Colombia](/wiki/Colombia "Colombia"), with an estimated 2,280,522 residents in 2023.[[5]](#cite_note-5) The city spans 560.3 km2 (216.3 sq mi) with 120.9 km2 (46.7 sq mi) of urban area,[[6]](#cite_note-6) making Cali the second-largest city in the country by area and the [third most populous](/wiki/List_of_cities_and_towns_in_Colombia "List of cities and towns in Colombia"). Cali is the main urban and economic center in the south of the country, and has one of Colombia's fastest-growing economies.[[7]](#cite_note-7)[[8]](#cite_note-8)[[9]](#cite_note-9) The city was founded on 25 July 1536 by the Spanish explorer [Sebastián de Belalcázar](/wiki/Sebasti%C3%A1n_de_Belalc%C3%A1zar "Sebastián de Belalcázar").

As a sporting center for Colombia, it was the host city for the [1971 Pan American Games](/wiki/1971_Pan_American_Games "1971 Pan American Games").[[10]](#cite_note-10) Cali also hosted the 1992 World Wrestling Championships, the [2013 edition](/wiki/World_Games_2013 "World Games 2013") of the [World Games](/wiki/World_Games "World Games"), the [UCI Track Cycling World Championships](/wiki/UCI_Track_Cycling_World_Championships "UCI Track Cycling World Championships") in 2014, the [World Youth Championships in Athletics](/wiki/IAAF_World_Youth_Championships_in_Athletics "IAAF World Youth Championships in Athletics") in 2015 as well as the inaugural [Junior Pan American Games](/wiki/2021_Junior_Pan_American_Games "2021 Junior Pan American Games") in 2021 and the [2022 World Athletics U20 Championships](/wiki/2022_World_Athletics_U20_Championships "2022 World Athletics U20 Championships").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

## Etymology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cali&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Etymology")]

Cali is the shortened form of the official name of the city: Santiago de Cali. "Santiago" honors [Saint James](/wiki/James,_son_of_Zebedee "James, son of Zebedee") whose [feast day](/wiki/Feast_day "Feast day") is celebrated on 25 July. The origin of the word "Cali" comes from the local Amerindians, the "[Calima](/wiki/Calima_culture "Calima culture")".[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### Pre-Columbian era and settlers

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cali&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Pre-Columbian era and settlers")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/19/Calima_-_Ceremonial_Tweezers_-_Walters_57262.jpg/250px-Calima_-_Ceremonial_Tweezers_-_Walters_57262.jpg)](/wiki/File:Calima_-_Ceremonial_Tweezers_-_Walters_57262.jpg)

Calima culture gold ceremonial tweezers from [Walters Art Museum](/wiki/Walters_Art_Museum "Walters Art Museum").

Before the arrival of the Spaniards, the region was inhabited by indigenous tribes, mostly speakers of [Cariban languages](/wiki/Cariban_languages "Cariban languages"). In the region between the [Cauca River](/wiki/Cauca_River "Cauca River") and the Western Cordillera, the Gorrones established themselves between the present day [Roldanillo](/wiki/Roldanillo "Roldanillo") and Santiago de Cali. The biggest town of the Morrones was sited on the River Pescador near the present-day towns of [Zarzal](/wiki/Zarzal "Zarzal") and [Bugalagrande](/wiki/Bugalagrande "Bugalagrande"). The Morrones traded with the [Quimbayas](/wiki/Quimbaya_civilization "Quimbaya civilization") who inhabited the north of the [Valle del Cauca](/wiki/Valle_del_Cauca "Valle del Cauca").

On his way to Cali, [Sebastián de Belalcázar](/wiki/Sebasti%C3%A1n_de_Belalc%C3%A1zar "Sebastián de Belalcázar") first met the Timbas who ran away before the arrival of the men, leaving behind gold. After the Timbas, towards the north, the Spaniards entered the territory of the chief Jamundí and his tribe, the Jamundíes, between the rivers Pance and [Jamundí](/wiki/Jamund%C3%AD "Jamundí"). This tribe offered a strong resistance to the invaders, fighting with poisonous darts and arrows against the [arquebuses](/wiki/Arquebus "Arquebus") and swords of the Spaniards. Eventually, the Spanish prevailed in the struggle over the central valley.

Before taking control over the region, the Spaniards had to defeat the chief Petecuy, whose tribe inhabited the area between the river Lilí and the Western Cordillera. Petecuy formed a large army with many tribes and fought the Spaniards on [Holy Tuesday](/wiki/Holy_Tuesday "Holy Tuesday") of 1536. The natives lost to the Spaniards and the region was divided in *[encomiendas](/wiki/Encomienda "Encomienda")*.

Santiago de Cali was important for Belalcázar because it was outside the [Inca Empire](/wiki/Inca_Empire "Inca Empire"). After the capture and execution of the Inca [Atahualpa](/wiki/Atahualpa "Atahualpa") at [Cajamarca](/wiki/Cajamarca "Cajamarca"), [Francisco Pizarro](/wiki/Francisco_Pizarro "Francisco Pizarro") had sent Belalcázar to take possession of [Guayaquil](/wiki/Guayaquil "Guayaquil") and Quito on his behalf, but Cali, being outside the Quechua empire, was claimed by Belalcazar as his own territory. After his death, his descendants maintained possession of much of the land until the [war of independence against Spain](/wiki/Patria_Boba "Patria Boba").

### Founding and colonial period

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cali&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Founding and colonial period")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/a/ab/Sebastian_de_belalcazar_fundador.jpg)](/wiki/File:Sebastian_de_belalcazar_fundador.jpg)

[Sebastián de Belalcázar](/wiki/Sebasti%C3%A1n_de_Belalc%C3%A1zar "Sebastián de Belalcázar").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a3/La_Merced.JPG/250px-La_Merced.JPG)](/wiki/File:La_Merced.JPG)

La Merced.

The founder of Cali, [Sebastián de Belalcázar](/wiki/Sebasti%C3%A1n_de_Belalc%C3%A1zar "Sebastián de Belalcázar"), came to the American continent in the third voyage made by Columbus in 1498. In 1532, after serving in [Darién](/wiki/Dari%C3%A9n_Province "Darién Province") and Nicaragua, he joined [Francisco Pizarro](/wiki/Francisco_Pizarro "Francisco Pizarro") in the conquest of Perú. In 1534, Belalcázar separated from Pizarro's expedition to find the city of [Quito](/wiki/Quito "Quito"), and later in his search of [El Dorado](/wiki/El_Dorado "El Dorado") he entered the territory of what is now Colombia, founding the cities of [Pasto](/wiki/Pasto,_Colombia "Pasto, Colombia") and [Popayán](/wiki/Popay%C3%A1n "Popayán").

Belalcázar founded Santiago de Cali on 25 July 1536, a few kilometres north of its present location, near what are now the towns of [Vijes](/wiki/Vijes "Vijes") and [Riofrío](/wiki/Riofr%C3%ADo,_Valle_del_Cauca "Riofrío, Valle del Cauca"). Under the orders of Belalcázar, captain Miguel Muñoz moved the city to its present location in 1537, where the chaplain Brother Santos de Añasco celebrated a mass in the place occupied by the Church La Merced today, and Belalcázar designated Pedro de Ayala as the first municipal authority.

During the *Colonia* (colonial period), Santiago de Cali was part of the *gobernación* of Popayán, which was part of [Quito](/wiki/Quito "Quito")'s Audiencia. Although initially Cali was the capital of Popayán's *gobernación*, Belalcázar moved this function to Popayán in 1540, owing to "better" weather there.

Until the 18th century, most of the territory of what is now Santiago de Cali was occupied by haciendas (cattle farms and plantations of food, with some sugar cane), and the city was only a small town near the [Cali River](/wiki/Cali_River "Cali River"). In 1793, Santiago de Cali had 6,548 inhabitants, 1,106 of whom were (African) slaves. The haciendas were the property of the dominant noble class with many slaves dedicated mostly to stockbreeding and raising sugar cane crops. Many of these haciendas became zones of the present city: Cañaveralejo, Chipichape, Pasoancho, Arroyohondo, Cañasgordas, Limonar and Meléndez. Santiago de Cali was strategically positioned for trade, centrally located in relation to the mining regions of [Antioquia](/wiki/Antioquia_Department "Antioquia Department"), [Chocó](/wiki/Department_of_Choc%C3%B3 "Department of Chocó"), and Popayán. The first trail for mules and horses between Santiago de Cali and the port of [Buenaventura](/wiki/Buenaventura,_Colombia "Buenaventura, Colombia") was completed in the colonial period.

### Independence

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cali&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Independence")]

On 3 July 1810, Santiago de Cali refused to recognize the [Council of Regency](/wiki/Junta_(Peninsular_War) "Junta (Peninsular War)") of Spain, and established its own junta. This local uprising predates the national one in [Bogotá](/wiki/Bogot%C3%A1 "Bogotá") by 17 days.[[11]](#cite_note-11) The Governor of [Popayán](/wiki/Popay%C3%A1n "Popayán"), [Miguel Tacón](/wiki/Miguel_Tac%C3%B3n_y_Rosique "Miguel Tacón y Rosique") organized an army to control the uprising. The people from Cali called for help to the "Junta Suprema" in Bogotá, which sent a contingent under Colonel Antonio Baraya to support the independence cause. For mutual defense, Cali also formed, with Anserma, [Cartago](/wiki/Cartago,_Colombia "Cartago, Colombia"), Toro, [Buga](/wiki/Buga,_Valle_del_Cauca "Buga, Valle del Cauca") and Caloto, the Confederated cities of the Cauca Valley, which [declared independence](/wiki/Patria_Boba "Patria Boba") from the [Governorate](/wiki/Governorate "Governorate") of Popayán on 1 February 1811, although they continued to recognize the absent [Ferdinand VII](/wiki/Ferdinand_VII_of_Spain "Ferdinand VII of Spain") as their [head of state](/wiki/Head_of_state "Head of state"). On 28 March 1811 in the battle of Bajo Palacé, the first in Colombia's Independence, the royalist Spanish army was defeated by the revolutionary army commanded by colonel Antonio Baraya with a detachment of 120 soldiers from his native Cundinamarca and a huge garrison of 1,080 men from Valle del Cauca led by brothers [Miguel and Francisco Cabal Barona](/w/index.php?title=Miguel_and_Francisco_Cabal_Barona&action=edit&redlink=1 "Miguel and Francisco Cabal Barona (page does not exist)").[[12]](#cite_note-12)

In the following years there were many battles between royalists and local militia. After having been released from captivity by Napoleon, Ferdinand VII sent a large army under the command of the "*Pacificador*" (Pacifier) [Pablo Morillo](/wiki/Pablo_Morillo "Pablo Morillo") who restored royalist rule in the area by 1816.

In 1819, after [Simón Bolívar](/wiki/Sim%C3%B3n_Bol%C3%ADvar "Simón Bolívar") defeated the bulk of the royalist army in the [Battle of Boyacá](/wiki/Battle_of_Boyac%C3%A1 "Battle of Boyacá"), there were new uprisings in the Valle del Cauca and the [Criollos](/wiki/Criollo_people "Criollo people") took control permanently. In 1822, Bolívar arrived in Santiago de Cali. The city was an important military outpost and the region contributed many men to the war of independence that liberated the nations in the south.

### Modernism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cali&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Modernism")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e0/Cali_theatre.JPG/250px-Cali_theatre.JPG)](/wiki/File:Cali_theatre.JPG)

Municipal Theater

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c9/Mapa-cali-1880s-WEB.jpg/250px-Mapa-cali-1880s-WEB.jpg)](/wiki/File:Mapa-cali-1880s-WEB.jpg)

Map of Cali in about 1882–1884, Spanish edition.

In the 19th century Cali, capital of the Valley of the Cauca department, was a quiet community with no more than 20,000 inhabitants. The urban center of the city was around the neighborhoods of Altozano and San Antonio.

Outside the city there were [mango](/wiki/Mango "Mango") plantations, pastures and communal land that were transferred from the Spanish Crown to the working classes. From the market gardens on this land the city was supplied with food. The economy centered around livestock, sugar cane, [panela](/wiki/Panela "Panela") (jaggery; a sugar derivative), cheese and gold mined in the Pacific. There was also a growing industrial and financial sector.

Jewish entrepreneurs came during the 18th and 19th centuries and achieved prominent positions in the city. Some married local women and felt they had to diminish or abandon their identity. These included the De Lima, Salazar, Espinoza, Arias, Ramirez, Perez and Lobo families from the Caribbean, as well as the author [Jorge Isaacs](/wiki/Jorge_Isaacs "Jorge Isaacs") of English Jewish ancestry, and the industrialist [James Martin Eder](/wiki/James_Martin_Eder "James Martin Eder") (who adopted the more Christian name of Santiago Eder when he translated his name to Spanish) born into the [Latvian Jewish](/wiki/Latvian_Jew "Latvian Jew") community. Over the generations most of their descendants were raised as secular Christians.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9f/V._de_Valenzuela%2C_R._Reyes_Jr.%2C_Gen._R._Reyes%2C_and_J._M._Eder.jpg/250px-V._de_Valenzuela%2C_R._Reyes_Jr.%2C_Gen._R._Reyes%2C_and_J._M._Eder.jpg)](/wiki/File:V._de_Valenzuela,_R._Reyes_Jr.,_Gen._R._Reyes,_and_J._M._Eder.jpg)

[James Martin Eder](/wiki/James_Martin_Eder "James Martin Eder"). Colombian Jewish entrepreneur.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8a/Fi_322_Isaacs%2C_Jorge.jpg/250px-Fi_322_Isaacs%2C_Jorge.jpg)](/wiki/File:Fi_322_Isaacs,_Jorge.jpg)

Portrait of [Jorge Isaacs](/wiki/Jorge_Isaacs "Jorge Isaacs"). Colombian Jewish writer and intellectual.

Around 1890 the Company of Public Works of Cauca, a private venture, built the market plaza. This spurred commercial development and it transformed into the Plaza Mayor or Plaza de Caycedo. In 1921, the market was sold to the Cali municipality. Very close to the 9th street was the principal station of the *[tranvia](/wiki/Tram "Tram")* of Cali, a system of public transportation that linked the city with suburban areas.

### Recent history

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cali&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Recent history")]

On 7 August 1956, at around 1 a.m., seven [Colombian army](/wiki/Colombian_army "Colombian army") trucks filled with 42 [tons](/wiki/Ton "Ton") of [dynamite](/wiki/Dynamite "Dynamite") exploded near the train station, destroying eight city blocks.[[13]](#cite_note-13)[[14]](#cite_note-14) The nearby army barracks was instantly destroyed. Windows were shattered for miles. More than 1,000 people were killed and several thousand injured (see [Cali explosion](/wiki/Cali_explosion "Cali explosion")).[[15]](#cite_note-15) The following year, the government decided to organize a fair in order to lift up the spirits of citizens as well as generate economic reactivation, which came to be known as the [Cali Fair](/wiki/Cali_Fair "Cali Fair").

In 1971, Santiago de Cali hosted the [Pan American Games](/wiki/Pan_American_Games "Pan American Games"), an event which is considered by many as the height of the city's golden age as a model of civic orderliness: following it, Cali was named the Sports Capital of Colombia. In 1982, the government of Cali inaugurated what is now the city's largest building and the third-largest in the Republic of Colombia—"La Torre de Cali", or The Cali Tower. It stands 42 stories tall and houses a hotel, offices and apartment complexes.

Cali became a focus center of the [2021 Colombian protests](/wiki/2021_Colombian_protests "2021 Colombian protests"), which started on 28 April 2021. While most demonstrations held in the city were peaceful, there was also strong rioting, looting, and clashes between protesters and police officers. The statue of Spanish conquistador and founder of the city [Sebastián de Belalcázar](/wiki/Sebasti%C3%A1n_de_Belalc%C3%A1zar "Sebastián de Belalcázar") was torn down by [Misak](/wiki/Misak_people "Misak people") protesters,[[16]](#cite_note-16) while several buses and stations of the mass transit system [MIO](/wiki/Masivo_Integrado_de_Occidente "Masivo Integrado de Occidente") were vandalized and burned,[[17]](#cite_note-17)[[18]](#cite_note-18) causing the MIO network to be destroyed by 60%.[[19]](#cite_note-19) Reports of multiple human rights violations and police abuse in the city were received by organizations such as [Human Rights Watch](/wiki/Human_Rights_Watch "Human Rights Watch").[[20]](#cite_note-20) During the night and early morning of 3 May 2021, 5 people died and 33 were injured due to clashes between protesters and the police and ESMAD in the city.[[21]](#cite_note-21) In the Siloé neighborhood, a peaceful demonstration was violently broken up by members of the public force,[[22]](#cite_note-22) with that sector being also affected by an unexpected internet crash, which occurred twice on 4 and 5 May.[[23]](#cite_note-23)

## Geography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cali&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Geography")]

Cali is located on the Cauca Valley to the west of the [Cauca River](/wiki/Cauca_River "Cauca River") and to the east of the Western Mountain Range near the hills known as [Farallones de Cali](/wiki/Farallones_de_Cali "Farallones de Cali"). The city rests approximately 1,000 metres (3,281 feet) [above sea level](/wiki/Above_mean_sea_level "Above mean sea level"). Approximately 100 kilometres (62 miles) west of Cali and over the Western Cordillera, lies the port city of [Buenaventura](/wiki/Buenaventura,_Colombia "Buenaventura, Colombia") on the Colombian Pacific coast. To the northeast are the manufacturing center of Yumbo and Cali's international airport, the [Alfonso Bonilla Aragón](/wiki/Alfonso_Bonilla_Arag%C3%B3n_International_Airport "Alfonso Bonilla Aragón International Airport") (CLO). It is Colombia's third-largest airport in terms of passengers, transporting 2,667,526 in 2009.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2c/Los_Farallones.jpg/960px-Los_Farallones.jpg)](/wiki/File:Los_Farallones.jpg)

Panoramic [Farallones de Cali](/wiki/Farallones_de_Cali "Farallones de Cali")

Santiago de Cali is located in a valley. The city is completely bordered by mountains to the West; the [Farallones de Cali](/wiki/Farallones_de_Cali "Farallones de Cali") mountains are the closest to the city. The Eastern part of the city is bordered by the Cauca River. To the north and south are extended plains. In the first one you can find the industrial city of [Yumbo](/wiki/Yumbo "Yumbo") which is part of Cali's metropolitan area, to the south you can find [Jamundí](/wiki/Jamund%C3%AD "Jamundí"), also part of the metropolitan area. The city is mainly flat, but there are areas mostly to the west that are mountainous, like San Antonio and La loma de la Cruz, which are both tourist sites. There are several rivers that descend from the Western Mountain Range and empty into the Cauca River, passing through the metropolitan area of Cali. In the western part of the city the Aguacatal River flows into the [Cali River](/wiki/Cali_River "Cali River"), which continues on to the [Cauca River](/wiki/Cauca_River "Cauca River"). In the south the rivers Cañaveralejo, Lilí, and Meléndez flow into the south channel which also empties into the Cauca River. Farther south, the banks of the [Pance River](/wiki/Pance_River "Pance River") are a popular place for recreation and leisure.

### Climate

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cali&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Climate")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0d/Tree_in_Cali%2C_Colombia.jpg/250px-Tree_in_Cali%2C_Colombia.jpg)](/wiki/File:Tree_in_Cali,_Colombia.jpg)

[Guaiacum officinale](/wiki/Guaiacum_officinale "Guaiacum officinale") tree flowered in Cali.

Under [Köppen's climate classification](/wiki/K%C3%B6ppen_climate_classification "Köppen climate classification"), Cali features a dry-summer [tropical monsoon climate](/wiki/Tropical_monsoon_climate "Tropical monsoon climate") ([Köppen climate classification](/wiki/K%C3%B6ppen_climate_classification "Köppen climate classification"): Am), bordering a [tropical savanna climate](/wiki/Tropical_savanna_climate "Tropical savanna climate") (Köppen: As). The Western Mountain Range rises from an average of 2,000 m (6,562 ft) [above sea level](/wiki/Above_mean_sea_level "Above mean sea level") in the northern part of the city to approximately 4,000 m (13,123 ft) to the south. Because of this variation in altitude, the weather in the northwest portion of the city is drier than in the southwest. The average annual precipitation varies between 900 and 1,800 mm (35 and 71 in) depending on the metropolitan zone for a citywide average of approximately 1,483 mm (58 in). Even though there is enough rainfall to make for lush surroundings, Cali is still in a strong [rain shadow](/wiki/Rain_shadow "Rain shadow"). Its closest seaport of [Buenaventura](/wiki/Buenaventura,_Valle_del_Cauca#Climate "Buenaventura, Valle del Cauca") about 80 kilometres (50 mi) away[[24]](#cite_note-24) is among the rainiest locations in the world. This is due to Buenaventura being on the windward side of the coastal mountains that in return shield Cali from the Pacific monsoons. Cali's average temperature is 24 °C (75 °F) with an average low temperature of 17.4 °C (63 °F) and a high of 30.5 °C (87 °F).

Owing to its proximity to the [equator](/wiki/Equator "Equator"), there are no major seasonal variations in Cali like winter, spring, summer and fall. However, locals refer to the dry season as the city's "summer", in which temperatures can rise to 30 to 34 °C (86 to 93 °F) and go down to 15 to 17 °C (59 to 63 °F) at night. During the rainy season (or "winter") temperatures can rise to 28 to 30 °C (82 to 86 °F) and go down to 18 to 20 °C (64 to 68 °F) at night. There are typically two rainy seasons: from March to May and from October to November. However, rain can be expected to fall at any point during the year, nourishing the city's permanent green and lush vegetation. The highest temperature ever recorded was 36.5 °C (98 °F) in July 1997, and the lowest temperature ever recorded was 13.4 °C (56 °F) in August 1979.[[25]](#cite_note-IDEAM2-25)

### Historic district

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cali&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Historic district")]

Santiago de Cali offers historical areas with cultural variety and other attractions. In downtown Cali there are many historic churches such as La Merced and La Ermita. Cali contains a well-preserved historical center. The most important zone is La plaza de Cayzedo, considered the center of the city, which is a square surrounded by many historical and modern buildings like El edificio Otero, La Catedral and El Palacio de Justicia. This plaza is close to other tourist places, like The Saint Francis church (in Spanish, *Iglesia de San Francisco*), the municipal theater and La Merced church. The city is also rich in monuments, parks, squares and museums. The most emblematic sculptures are [Cristo Rey](/wiki/Cristo_Rey_(Colombian_statue) "Cristo Rey (Colombian statue)"), located upon a mountain range; Sebastian de Belalcazar, founder of the city; and Las tres Cruces, a place of pilgrimage during the days of the Holy Week.

Some of Cali's main tourist centers include:

* Plaza de Caicedo: The main square of the city, located in downtown Cali. The plaza is named after Cali's hero, Joaquín de Caicedo y Cuero.
* Parque del perro: Located in the *San Fernando* neighborhood, this is one of the most popular areas in the city. It is full of restaurants and bars. The park gets its name because it has a dog monument in the middle of it.
* Avenida San Joaquín: Located in the *[Ciudad Jardín](/wiki/Ciudad_Jard%C3%ADn,_Cali "Ciudad Jardín, Cali")*, it is a popular place in the city.
* Sebastian de Belalcázar's monument: It is the most visited and popular monument in the city[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] located in the hills overlooking the city. The monument is famous due to its pointing finger which is pointing to the opposite direction of the valley, while its face is looking down at the city.
* Cali River: The river is located in the west and is surrounded by restaurants, hotels and museums like [La Tertulia](/wiki/La_Tertulia_Museum "La Tertulia Museum"), an art museum.
* [El Gato del Río](/wiki/El_Gato_del_R%C3%ADo "El Gato del Río"): Located next to the river, a sculpture of a cat by [Hernando Tejada](/wiki/Hernando_Tejada "Hernando Tejada")[[29]](#cite_note-29)
* Cristo Rey: A religious monument located on a hill that offers the best view of the city. It is 31 m high, of which 5 m belong to the base.[[30]](#cite_note-30)[[31]](#cite_note-31)
* [Boulevard del río Cali](/w/index.php?title=Boulevard_del_r%C3%ADo_Cali&action=edit&redlink=1 "Boulevard del río Cali (page does not exist)"): (Colombia Avenue Boulevard) is located by the Cali river side in the historic center of the city of Santiago de Cali, Colombia. It carries the name of the avenue where vehicles used to transit but today is the boulevard where there is a tunnel located directly below it. This boulevard is about 980 meters long.
* San Antonio: This is the most traditional neighborhood of Cali. In the colonial age, it was the extension of the downtown. Although it was split off by 'La calle Quinta' (Fifth street). The entire neighborhood is on a hill, and at the top you will find the San Antonio Park.
* Juanchito: Host to a large number of night clubs dedicated to salsa music, motels offering hourly rates, and adjacent to some of the poorest areas of the city. Technically, it belongs to another municipality, but is often visited by and associated with citizens of Cali.
* Farallones de Cali: A part of the city sits on hills belonging to the Colombian western mountain chain. Beyond these hills is Farallones de Cali national park.
* La Ceiba: A large and old Ceiba tree on a street corner at the west of the city.[[32]](#cite_note-32)
* Orquideorama Enrique Perez Arbeláez: This wooded park, at AV 2 N #48-10, is the site of the large orchid show hosted each fall by Asociacion Vallecaucana de Orquideologia.[[33]](#cite_note-33) In 2011, the show was to be held from 21 to 25 September and was to include international judging by the American Orchid Society.[[34]](#cite_note-34) It is also a good place for birdwatching.
* Cerro de las Tres Cruces: The Three Crosses monument sits at 1,480 m above sea level and is located in the NW part of the city. The central cross is the largest sitting at 26 m tall and 11 m wide. The two crosses besides the main cross have a height of 22 m and a width of 8 m. Visitors are known to hike the mountain and can enjoy the view of the city, a freshly squeezed orange/carrot juice, and an outdoor gym before hiking back down.[[35]](#cite_note-35)

### Medical tourism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cali&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Medical tourism")]

According to recent [Lonely Planet](/wiki/Lonely_Planet "Lonely Planet")[[36]](#cite_note-36) guides of Colombia, Cali has recently become famous for being a prime destination for people seeking cheap cosmetic surgery and aesthetician centers. The city is very well known as The Capital of the Plastic Surgeries. There have been no significant reports of problems in this area, but Lonely Planet advises proper research before pursuing such an idea. About 50,000 cosmetic surgery procedures occurred in Cali in 2010, of which around 14,000 involved patients from abroad.[[37]](#cite_note-37)

## Transport

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cali&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Transport")]

### Airports

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cali&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Airports")]

Santiago de Cali is served by [Alfonso Bonilla Aragón International Airport](/wiki/Alfonso_Bonilla_Arag%C3%B3n_International_Airport "Alfonso Bonilla Aragón International Airport") ([IATA](/wiki/IATA_airport_code "IATA airport code"): **CLO**, [ICAO](/wiki/ICAO_airport_code "ICAO airport code"): **SKCL**), located in the City of Palmira. It is Colombia's third largest airport in terms of passengers (transporting 3,422,919 in 2010) and fourth in cargo. Alfonso Bonilla Aragón is located in a long, narrow valley that runs from north to south, and is surrounded by mountains up to 14,000 feet (4,300 m) high. The airport is connected to the city by a highway known as the "recta a Palmira", that in the last few years has been upgraded to make the airport more accessible to Cali and the surrounding city centers in the region. The airport has also been remodeled recently; some of the last significant events in those terms were the inauguration of the VIP room in the National terminal and the installation of a main electronic screen in the center of the check-in area.

The [Marco Fidel Suárez Air Base](/wiki/Marco_Fidel_Su%C3%A1rez_Air_Base "Marco Fidel Suárez Air Base") is a military airport close to the city's downtown. It is located in the east side of the city and belongs to the [Colombian Air Force](/wiki/Colombian_Air_Force "Colombian Air Force"). It is used as one of the main training centers for the country's [fighter pilots](/wiki/Fighter_pilot "Fighter pilot").

### Public transport

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cali&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Public transport")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d5/MIO_CALI_COLOMBIA.JPG/250px-MIO_CALI_COLOMBIA.JPG)](/wiki/File:MIO_CALI_COLOMBIA.JPG)

Masivo Integrado de Occidente (MIO)

The city of Cali offers a variety of ways to move through the city. In March 2009, The Masivo Integrado de Occidente (MIO) began operations. It is planned to be the primary system that connects the city. Non-MIO buses in various neighborhoods round out the system. Taxis are available.

[Masivo Integrado de Occidente](/wiki/Masivo_Integrado_de_Occidente "Masivo Integrado de Occidente") (MIO): It is a [bus rapid transit](/wiki/Bus_rapid_transit "Bus rapid transit") system of articulated buses that run on dedicated bus lanes in the middle of major thoroughfares, with stations connected to sidewalks by dedicated pedestrian crossings or bridges. The system layout is 243 kilometres (151 miles) distributed in trunk, pre-trunk and complementary corridors. The system also integrated the renovation and recuperation of the public space. The MIO system was not only designed for the public transport, but built for public use with extensive new sidewalks, parks, gardens and public squares for the public to enjoy.

The system also includes a [cable car](/wiki/Aerial_lift "Aerial lift") line named [MIO Cable](/wiki/MIO_Cable "MIO Cable"), which is fully integrated with the MIO network and directly serves the residents of the Siloé district.

More information about El MIO is in the official web site of Metrocali.[[38]](#cite_note-38)

### Bus Central Station

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cali&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Bus Central Station")]

Cali is served by over 20 coach companies[[39]](#cite_note-39) which gather in the Central Bus Station.[[40]](#cite_note-40) The Station is centrally located near the old (now redundant) railway station, which serves now as Metrocali's headquarters. Depending on the company and the destination, the vehicles range from [minibuses](/wiki/Minibus "Minibus") to large [coaches](/wiki/Coach_(vehicle) "Coach (vehicle)"). Recently, in their last float renovation wave, Colombian coaches operators have opted for Brazil's [Marcopolo](/wiki/Marcopolo_S.A. "Marcopolo S.A.") buses. Informal stops exist for the short destination rides all along the way from Central Station to the town of destination. In some cases, it is enough for a passenger to ask to get off the bus for the bus to stop.

### Public transportation statistics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cali&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Public transportation statistics")]

The average amount of time people spend commuting with public transit in Cali, for example to and from work, on a weekday is 88 min. 23% of public transit riders, ride for more than 2 hours every day. The average amount of time people wait at a stop or station for public transit is 21 min, while 43% of riders wait for over 20 minutes on average every day. The average distance people usually ride in a single trip with public transit is 5.6 km, while 3% travel for over 12 km in a single direction.[[41]](#cite_note-41)

Cali and Valle del Cauca constitute the third largest center national and international economic exchange in Colombia. The city is an important stop on way to Ecuador, and is connected with the world through the seaport of Buenaventura.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/78/Jose_Serrano_modificado_Cali_edificio_Banco_de_Occidente.jpg/250px-Jose_Serrano_modificado_Cali_edificio_Banco_de_Occidente.jpg)](/wiki/File:Jose_Serrano_modificado_Cali_edificio_Banco_de_Occidente.jpg)

### Economic history

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cali&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Economic history")]

The economic transformation of Cali and the Valle del Cauca during the twentieth century and its crisis of the century, and the outlook to the new century have been the subject of deep analysis of financial and academic institutions. This section is based on the analysis and recommendations of the report *Cali Colombia – Toward a City Development and Strategy* published by the [World Bank](/wiki/World_Bank "World Bank") in 2002, and the Regional Economic Situation Reports (ICER) published quarterly by the [DANE](/wiki/Departamento_Administrativo_Nacional_de_Estad%C3%ADstica "Departamento Administrativo Nacional de Estadística").

In the early twentieth century the city's economy was concentrated in the production of sugar, based on an agricultural model in which large tracts of land were cultivated with minimum use of labor. As a result, a few families owned vast areas of land in one of the most fertile regions of the country. This was an important factor in determining the power relations and the organization of the city through the twentieth century.

In the period 1910–1930, the city's economy shifted its focus from an agricultural model to become a commercial node at the national level through the development of basic infrastructure such as construction of the railway to Buenaventura and the creation of the department of [Valle del Cauca](/wiki/Valle_del_Cauca "Valle del Cauca") with Cali designated as its capital.

Although the industrial vallecaucana revolution of Cali did not begin until the third decade of the twentieth century, some companies had already begun to build the industrial development of the region, as the printing company Carvajal y Cia (which began operations in 1904). In 1929 there is the creation of Soap Varela Hermanos, in the 1930s other industries begin to grow as large scale factories of [gas](/wiki/Fuel_gas "Fuel gas") and beer, printing and cigarettes. Smaller companies also appeared focusing on [textile clothing](/wiki/Textiles "Textiles"), [chemicals](/wiki/Chemical_industry "Chemical industry"), chocolates, [building materials](/wiki/Building_materials "Building materials"), leather articles and furniture.

In 1940, Cali had already ceased to be a single point of trade and its economy was focused on industry. A few years before the decade began a major investment of foreign capital led to the establishment of many factories and local business grew to multinational corporations; as Croydon in 1937, Cementos del Valle in 1939, and Carton Colombia in 1941; [Goodyear](/wiki/Goodyear_Tire_and_Rubber_Company "Goodyear Tire and Rubber Company") and [Colgate-Palmolive](/wiki/Colgate-Palmolive "Colgate-Palmolive") came from the US in 1941. Later came other corporations like General CEAT (Centelsa) in 1955, and pharmaceutical laboratories were based in the Cauca Valley between 1940 and 1960, as Tecnoquímicas and Baxter.

The flourishing industrial city attracted waves of immigration in the 1950s and 1960s. In these decades there were important partnerships between the public and private sectors, as productive sector support to programs of business administration at the [University of Valle](/wiki/University_of_Valle "University of Valle"). The growth of the university training professionals and technologists, as well as infrastructure development, were crucial for the further development of industry and trade in the Valle del Cauca. This trend continued in the 1970s and early years of the next decade. Public investment in infrastructure reached significant levels benefiting not only the production sector but also the growing population, this made Cali and Valle del Cauca models further development across the country.

In 1998, when the economic crisis became apparent, the national government could not respond to the call of the local politicians and mayors had to introduce austerity measures under pressure from [creditors](/wiki/Creditor "Creditor"), which caused the vallecaucano development model to collapse. Additionally, the tightening of the country's internal conflict required a tax increase aimed at national war spending, leaving less room for local governments to collect, through taxes, the money required for their development plans.

### Current statistics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cali&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Current statistics")]

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
|  | This section needs to be **updated**. Please help update this article to reflect recent events or newly available information. *(July 2022)* |

According to statistics by [DANE](/wiki/Departamento_Administrativo_Nacional_de_Estad%C3%ADstica "Departamento Administrativo Nacional de Estadística"), in 1995 the annual growth of GDP of the Valle del Cauca region was almost twice the national rate. For 1997, GDP increased marginally, with the region itself going up 1%. In 1999 the country's economic recession was felt through a depression that made the economy contract, showing GDP growth of 4%. Since then GDP has grown in the valley with ups and downs, but its percentage share nationwide has been falling since 1995 as shown in the graph.

The department contributes significantly to the national economy. According to statistics for the year 2005, for agriculture the valley contributes 5.37% of the national production, which is relatively low compared with [Antioquia](/wiki/Antioquia_Department "Antioquia Department") (15.48%) or [Cundinamarca](/wiki/Cundinamarca_department "Cundinamarca department") (12.81%). In fisheries products, the region ranks first, with the region contributing 36% of the country's total production. As for mining, the valley is not a metal-producing region; however, in terms of non-metallic minerals, the department contributes 8.15% of the total [value added](/wiki/Value_added "Value added") across Colombia.

The regional [industries](/wiki/Industrial_sector "Industrial sector") contribute 13.81% of the national value-added tax, second only to Bogotá with 25.39% and 18.20% in Antioquia. Particularly, the industries of food, beverages, and snuff are important products in the valley's economy, contributing 16% of the national value added, equaled or surpassed only by Antioquia and Bogotá. On trade, nationally Bogotá has 32.22%, Antioquia 13.25%, and Valle 11.34%. In the valley transportation services provide 12.52% of value added.

The Consumer Price Index ([IPC](/wiki/Consumer_price_index "Consumer price index")) of Cali has been since the last decade one of the lowest among Colombian cities. About 78% of Cali's people are of working age (over 18 years). In 2005, for the first time in six years, the city had an occupancy rate above 60%, which confirms the good state of the economy, led primarily by growth in manufacturing, agriculture, and trade, among others.

Drogas La Rebaja, one of Colombia's largest pharmacy store chains, is based in Cali.So is candy company [Colombina](/wiki/Colombina_(candy_brand) "Colombina (candy brand)"). A bank, Banco de Occidente, a Grupo Aval company, has its head office in Cali.

## Public order

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cali&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Public order")]

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
|  | This section needs to be **updated**. Please help update this article to reflect recent events or newly available information. *(July 2022)* |

Cali had a murder rate of 66.4 per 100,000 inhabitants in 2014; this fell to 51.5 per 100,000 inhabitants in 2017.[[42]](#cite_note-42)[[43]](#cite_note-43) In 2006 the budget for security in [Medellín](/wiki/Medell%C3%ADn "Medellín") was more than four times higher than in Cali, and in Bogota (which is 3.5 times more populous) the budget was more than seven times higher.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Between 1 January and 1 December 2011 there were 1870[[44]](#cite_note-44) intentional homicides in the municipality of Cali, which is considered a 5% increase compared to 2010.[[45]](#cite_note-45) The surge in violence in Cali in 2011 has partly been attributed to what has been described as an ongoing 'mafia war' between the 'neo-paramilitary' groups [Los Rastrojos](/wiki/Los_Rastrojos "Los Rastrojos") and [Los Urabeños](/wiki/Los_Urabe%C3%B1os "Los Urabeños"), both involved in drug trafficking.[[46]](#cite_note-46) Los Rastrojos are considered the 'heirs' of the [Cali Cartel](/wiki/Cali_Cartel "Cali Cartel") and Los Urabeños have their roots in Colombia's coast. Los Rastrojos are accused of committing at least 80 murders in Cali in 2011.[[47]](#cite_note-47) According to Colombia's most influential weekly magazine, *[Semana](/wiki/Semana "Semana")*, there are over 1,700 assassins working for various groups in the city.[[48]](#cite_note-semana.com-48) 3.8 percent of street addresses account for 100 percent of homicides.[[49]](#cite_note-49)

During 2012, there was a decrease in homicides, with 24 cases fewer than for the same period last year, and there were 294 fewer people injured, which means a reduction of 8% from a year previous. There were 323 fewer car thefts, i.e. a decrease of 21% over the same period of 2011. There were 152 fewer residential burglaries, which means a reduction of 17% from the same period of 2011. There was also strengthening in technology citywide by the installation of 254 security cameras.

## Politics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cali&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Politics")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cd/Consejo_de_cali.JPG/250px-Consejo_de_cali.JPG)](/wiki/File:Consejo_de_cali.JPG)

Concejo de Cali (City council).

Cali is governed by a mayor, elected to a four-year term. Several administrative departments and secretaries report to the mayor. [Mayoral elections](/w/index.php?title=Elecciones_regionales_en_Colombia&action=edit&redlink=1 "Elecciones regionales en Colombia (page does not exist)") started in 1986, followed by elections for governors in 1992. Previously, all Colombian regional executive-branch leaders were appointed by the [President of Colombia](/wiki/President_of_Colombia "President of Colombia"). The first elected mayor was [Carlos Holmes Trujillo](/wiki/Carlos_Holmes_Trujillo "Carlos Holmes Trujillo") of the Liberal Party. Unlike other Colombian cities, Cali has not properly adapted to the new mayoral election system[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]. The city has experienced unsuccessful terms under some of its elected mayors, two of which ended up being removed from office.

## Education

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cali&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Education")]

A large part of the population relies on the public educational system, which is underfunded and in some cases improperly managed. Schools are under municipal or departmental management, the former being the most common. The Municipal Secretary of Education manages a large part of the city's budget, which has brought some politicians to try to control it as their personal organization.

The city is endowed with the most sophisticated and high-quality secondary education institutions and universities in the region. Most universities are located in the south part of the city. Among the most prestigious are [University of Valle](/wiki/University_of_Valle "University of Valle") (Public), [Pontificia Universidad Javeriana](/wiki/Pontificia_Universidad_Javeriana "Pontificia Universidad Javeriana") (Private), and [Universidad Icesi](/wiki/Universidad_Icesi "Universidad Icesi") (Private).

Main article: [Cali Fair](/wiki/Cali_Fair "Cali Fair")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/53/Sals%C3%B3dromo%2C_Feria_de_Cali%2C_Colombia.JPG/250px-Sals%C3%B3dromo%2C_Feria_de_Cali%2C_Colombia.JPG)](/wiki/File:Sals%C3%B3dromo,_Feria_de_Cali,_Colombia.JPG)

La "[Feria de Cali](/wiki/Cali_Fair "Cali Fair")" (The Cali Fair), also known as La Feria de la Caña (Sugar Cane Fair), is the most important cultural event in the city, celebrated since 1957. It is celebrated every year from 25 to 30 December.[[68]](#cite_note-68) The fair is not only the most important event for the cultural identity of the citizens, but it also stimulates the economic development of the city by significantly increasing tourism and seasonal employment, providing opportunities for independent merchants to sell their products during the events, and encouraging the improvement of the city's infrastructure. Nowadays, the fair fhosts several national and international celebrities during the music festivals and concerts.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### Salsa music

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cali&action=edit&section=30 "Edit section: Salsa music")]

Cali is also known as the Salsa Capital "Capital de la [Salsa"](/wiki/Salsa_music "Salsa music") given the city's love for that genre of music.[[69]](#cite_note-69)[[70]](#cite_note-70)[[71]](#cite_note-71) In early July there is the Summer Salsa Festival which lasts for one week. It usually includes concerts by some of the world's great salsa bands as well as dance shows and "melomano" competitions in which salsa connoisseurs try to outdo each other by digging deep into the archives of salsa music and related sounds to find and reveal long lost tunes. On any night of the week small salsa clubs offer a variety of Afro and Caribbean beats.[[72]](#cite_note-72) Furthermore, the last Friday of every month, the city has an event known as "Delirio", already internationally known with the most spectacular salsa shows presented, "Las Vegas" style.

## Sports

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cali&action=edit&section=32 "Edit section: Sports")]

At a professional level, Cali hosts only [football](/wiki/Association_football "Association football") teams. At the amateur level there are Basketball, Football, Volleyball, and other sports. Nationally, Cali's athletes compete with Bogotá's and Medellín's in most sport tournaments and championships.

Cali has two main athletic events, a mid-year half marathon 1⁄2 Maraton de Cali[[73]](#cite_note-73) and a December 10k race called Carrera del Río Cali.

### Colombia's sports capital

Opening Ceremony of the 1971 Pan American Games

Santiago de Cali is recognized as the sports capital of Colombia. It is the first Colombian city to have hosted the Pan American Games (see [1971 Pan American Games](/wiki/1971_Pan_American_Games "1971 Pan American Games")), and has also won the National Olympic Games more than any other region in Colombia. The city also has one of the most developed sport infrastructures in the country. Many sporting events have taken place in the city.

Cali has one football stadium: [Estadio Olímpico Pascual Guerrero](/wiki/Estadio_Ol%C3%ADmpico_Pascual_Guerrero "Estadio Olímpico Pascual Guerrero"), which is currently home of [América de Cali](/wiki/Am%C3%A9rica_de_Cali "América de Cali"), [Atlético F.C.](/wiki/Atl%C3%A9tico_Cali "Atlético Cali"), and [Boca Juniors de Cali](/wiki/Boca_Juniors_de_Cali "Boca Juniors de Cali"), and was home of [Deportivo Cali](/wiki/Deportivo_Cali "Deportivo Cali") until 2015 when they moved to [Estadio Deportivo Cali](/wiki/Estadio_Deportivo_Cali "Estadio Deportivo Cali"), located in nearby [Palmira](/wiki/Palmira,_Valle_del_Cauca "Palmira, Valle del Cauca"). Deportivo Cali is the only Colombian football club that owns its stadium, since all other football stadiums in Colombia are government-owned. Other important places of sporting activity in the city are "[Coliseo El Pueblo](/wiki/Coliseo_El_Pueblo "Coliseo El Pueblo")", a covered arena center used for all types of events which hosted the [2016 FIFA Futsal World Cup](/wiki/2016_FIFA_Futsal_World_Cup "2016 FIFA Futsal World Cup"), and [Coliseo Evangelista Mora](/w/index.php?title=Coliseo_Evangelista_Mora&action=edit&redlink=1 "Coliseo Evangelista Mora (page does not exist)"), mainly used for basketball. Cali will host the inaugural [Junior Pan American Games](/wiki/2021_Junior_Pan_American_Games "2021 Junior Pan American Games") in 2021.

### Infrastructure

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cali&action=edit&section=34 "Edit section: Infrastructure")]

Cali's infrastructure has permitted it to host several major international sports competitions such as the 1971 Pan American Games, numerous Games of the Pacific, the final phase of the [1982 World basketball championship](/wiki/1982_FIBA_World_Championship "1982 FIBA World Championship"), the 1999 World's Roller Hockey Championships, women's basketball and swimming events, Pan American Speed-Track Cycling Championships, and most recently the World's Roller Speed Skating Championships. Cali was the host city of the [World Games 2013](/wiki/World_Games_2013 "World Games 2013").

### Football

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cali&action=edit&section=35 "Edit section: Football")]

Santiago de Cali is home to football clubs [Deportivo Cali](/wiki/Deportivo_Cali "Deportivo Cali") and [América de Cali](/wiki/Am%C3%A9rica_de_Cali "América de Cali"). Many well-known football players were either born in Cali or have played in one of its clubs. including Willington Ortiz, [Carlos Valderrama](/wiki/Carlos_Valderrama "Carlos Valderrama"), [Ántony de Ávila](/wiki/%C3%81ntony_de_%C3%81vila "Ántony de Ávila"), [Álex Escobar](/wiki/%C3%81lex_Escobar "Álex Escobar"), [Julio César Falcioni](/wiki/Julio_C%C3%A9sar_Falcioni "Julio César Falcioni"), [Jorge Orosmán da Silva](/wiki/Jorge_Orosm%C3%A1n_da_Silva "Jorge Orosmán da Silva"), [Jorge Bermúdez](/wiki/Jorge_Berm%C3%BAdez "Jorge Bermúdez"), [Giovanni Hernández](/wiki/Giovanni_Hern%C3%A1ndez "Giovanni Hernández"), [Hugo Rodallega](/wiki/Hugo_Rodallega "Hugo Rodallega"), [Mario Yepes](/wiki/Mario_Yepes "Mario Yepes"), [Faryd Mondragón](/wiki/Faryd_Mondrag%C3%B3n "Faryd Mondragón"), [Adolfo Valencia](/wiki/Adolfo_Valencia "Adolfo Valencia"), and [Óscar Córdoba](/wiki/%C3%93scar_C%C3%B3rdoba "Óscar Córdoba").

According to [CONMEBOL](/wiki/CONMEBOL "CONMEBOL"), América ranks second and Deportivo Cali third[[74]](#cite_note-74) in the Colombian national ranking, which ranks itself third in the CONMEBOL ranking. América was ranked as the world's second best club in 1996 by the IFFHS (International Federation of Football History & Statistics)[[75]](#cite_note-75) and 35th in the All-Time Club World Ranking of the IFFHS.[[76]](#cite_note-76) Cali is the Colombian city having hosted the most [Colombian first division](/wiki/Categor%C3%ADa_Primera_A "Categoría Primera A") finals, with 40 matches being played in the stadium. América has won 15 titles, and has been runner-up 7 times. Deportivo Cali has won 10 titles and ended as runner-up 14 times. They have played finals against each other three times. In the early 1950s, current second-tier team [Boca Juniors](/wiki/Boca_Juniors_de_Cali "Boca Juniors de Cali") lost two finals.

### Basketball and bullfighting

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cali&action=edit&section=36 "Edit section: Basketball and bullfighting")]

There is a basketball team in the city of Cali currently. It is known as Team cali.[[77]](#cite_note-77) In addition, basketball is the second-most played sport in the city. Basketball is a preferred sport at the city center location "La Carrera del Cholado". Football still surpasses basketball in sport popularity.
[Bullfighting](/wiki/Bullfighting "Bullfighting") is staged during the [Cali Fair](/wiki/Cali_Fair "Cali Fair") which is held in December. It is anticipated by many citizens in Cali, as well as by many people in all Colombia. The bullfighting [ring](/wiki/Bullring "Bullring") is called Plaza de Toros de Cañaveralejo, with a capacity of 16,000 and located in the southwest of the city.

### Other sports

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cali&action=edit&section=37 "Edit section: Other sports")]

[Rugby](/wiki/Rugby_football "Rugby football"), tennis, swimming, [archery](/wiki/Archery "Archery"), and [ultimate](/wiki/Ultimate_(sport) "Ultimate (sport)") are also played in Cali. The [IAAF World Youth Championships in Athletics](/wiki/IAAF_World_Youth_Championships_in_Athletics "IAAF World Youth Championships in Athletics") was hosted in Cali in July 2015.
Cali also hosted the 2015 Underwater Rugby World Cup.[[78]](#cite_note-78)
